questions.question: Explain the purpose of wait() system call in process management. What problem does it solve?
keyPoints: Suspends parent until child terminates
Prevents zombie processes by allowing parent to read exit status
Enables proper process cleanup and resource management

questions.question: Differentiate between zombie and orphan processes. How does the system handle each case?
keyPoints: Zombie: terminated child whose exit status not read by parent
Orphan: child whose parent terminated before child
Zombie: remains in process table until parent calls wait()
Orphan: adopted by init process which calls wait()

questions.question: Write the syntax and explain the purpose of waitpid() system call. How is it different from wait()?
keyPoints: waitpid(pid, status, options) syntax
Allows waiting for specific child by PID
Provides WNOHANG option for non-blocking wait
wait() is equivalent to waitpid(-1, &status, 0)

questions.question: Explain the following macros with examples: WIFEXITED, WEXITSTATUS, WIFSIGNALED
keyPoints: WIFEXITED: checks normal termination (exit/return)
WEXITSTATUS: extracts exit code after WIFEXITED true
WIFSIGNALED: checks if terminated by signal
Usage: if(WIFEXITED(status)) printf("Exit code: %d", WEXITSTATUS(status))

questions.question: What happens if a parent process does not call wait() after fork()? Explain with consequences.
keyPoints: Child becomes zombie on termination
Zombie occupies process table entry
Resource leak if many zombies created
Zombie remains until parent terminates or calls wait()
In extreme cases, system may run out of process slots

questions.question: Describe the process synchronization mechanism using wait() between parent and child processes.
keyPoints: Parent creates child via fork()
Parent calls wait() to suspend until child completes
Child does its task and exits with status
Parent wakes up, reads status, continues execution
Ensures proper sequencing: child completes before parent proceeds

questions.question: Explain how init process (PID 1) prevents orphan processes from becoming zombies.
keyPoints: init is the first process created by kernel
When parent dies, orphans are reparented to init
init continuously calls wait() in a loop
init reaps any terminated orphan children
This automatic cleanup prevents orphan zombies

questions.question: Write a C program demonstrating the use of wait() system call. The parent process should create a child process, the child should perform some operation and exit with a status code, and the parent should wait for the child and display the exit status.
keyPoints: Use fork() to create child process
Child: perform task (e.g., print, compute) and exit with status using exit()
Parent: call wait() to get child status
Use WIFEXITED to check normal termination
Use WEXITSTATUS to extract exit code
Proper error handling for fork() and wait()
Clean output showing parent waits and displays child's exit code

questions.question: Explain the wait() and waitpid() system calls in detail. Discuss their parameters, return values, and the various options available with waitpid(). Illustrate with scenarios when each would be preferred.
keyPoints: wait() syntax, parameters, return values
waitpid() syntax with pid and options
WNOHANG: non-blocking check
WUNTRACED: report stopped processes
Waiting for specific child vs any child
Handling multiple children with loop
Practical scenarios and trade-offs

questions.question: A process can terminate in different ways. Explain the various process termination scenarios and how wait() status macros help determine the termination method. Write pseudo-code to handle different termination cases.
keyPoints: Normal termination via exit() or return from main()
Abnormal termination via signal
Stopped by signal (job control)
Continued after stop
WIFEXITED/WIFSIGNALED/WIFSTOPPED/WIFCONTINUED macros
WEXITSTATUS for exit code
WTERMSIG for signal number
Pseudo-code handling each case
